focus: article changes (der/die/das variations), ein/kein and their variations, dative prepositions (vor, neben, an, etc.)

sections.title: What is it?
sections.content: German uses a system of grammatical cases to show the role each noun plays in a sentence. The case of a noun determines the form of its article — this is why der/die/das change. At A1 level, you need three cases: Nominativ (the subject), Akkusativ (the direct object), and Dativ (the indirect object or following certain prepositions).

sections.title: When to use each case
sections.content:
NOMINATIV — The subject: the person or thing performing the action.
Ask: "Who or what is doing this?"
Example: Der Mann schläft. (The man is sleeping.)

AKKUSATIV — The direct object: what is directly affected by the action.
Ask: "Who or what is being acted on?"
Example: Ich kaufe den Kaffee. (I buy the coffee.)

DATIV — The indirect object: to whom or for whom something is done. Also required by certain prepositions when expressing location.
Ask: "To whom? For whom? Where is it (location with a preposition)?"
Example: Ich gebe dem Mann das Buch. (I give the man the book.)

sections.title: How to form it — Definite Articles (der/die/das)
sections.content:
| Case       | Masculine (m) | Feminine (f) | Neuter (n) | Plural |
|------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Nominativ  | der  | die  | das  | die  |
| Akkusativ  | den  | die  | das  | die  |
| Dativ      | dem  | der  | dem  | den  |

What changes:
- Masculine changes in BOTH Akkusativ and Dativ: der → den → dem
- Feminine changes only in Dativ: die → der
- Neuter changes only in Dativ: das → dem
- Plural changes only in Dativ: die → den

Memory trick: In Akkusativ, ONLY masculine changes (der → den). In Dativ, ALL genders change: masculine and neuter both become dem, feminine becomes der.

sections.title: How to form it — Indefinite Articles (ein/eine)
sections.content:
| Case       | Masculine (m) | Feminine (f) | Neuter (n) |
|------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Nominativ  | ein   | eine  | ein   |
| Akkusativ  | einen | eine  | ein   |
| Dativ      | einem | einer | einem |

Note: There is no plural of ein — you simply use no article for plural indefinite nouns ("Ich kaufe Äpfel" — I am buying apples).

Key changes:
- Masculine Akkusativ: ein → einen
- All genders in Dativ gain a suffix: -em (masculine), -er (feminine), -em (neuter)

sections.title: How to form it — Negative Article (kein/keine)
sections.content:
kein/keine means 'not a / no' and follows the SAME pattern as ein/eine, but it also has a plural form:

| Case       | Masculine (m) | Feminine (f) | Neuter (n) | Plural  |
|------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Nominativ  | kein   | keine  | kein   | keine  |
| Akkusativ  | keinen | keine  | kein   | keine  |
| Dativ      | keinem | keiner | keinem | keinen |

Usage: Use kein/keine to negate a noun that would use ein/eine or no article at all.
- Ich habe ein Auto. → Ich habe kein Auto. (I don't have a car.)
- Ich habe Hunger. → Ich habe keinen Hunger. (I'm not hungry.)

Important: Do NOT use 'nicht' to negate a noun that takes ein — that is a common learner error. 'Nicht ein Auto' is wrong; 'kein Auto' is correct.

sections.title: Dative Prepositions — The Connection You Noticed
sections.content:
Your observation is exactly right. When a preposition forces dative case, the article changes to its dative form — it is the same case system at work.

ALWAYS DATIVE (at A1 level): mit, bei, zu, von, aus, nach, seit, gegenüber

TWO-WAY PREPOSITIONS — dative for location, accusative for direction:
an, auf, hinter, in, neben, über, unter, vor, zwischen
→ Use DATIV when answering: "Where is it?" (location — something is staying/being somewhere)
→ Use AKKUSATIV when answering: "Where is it going?" (direction/movement — something is moving to a place)

Examples:
- Das Restaurant ist neben dem Hotel. (Dativ: WHERE IS the restaurant? → location)
- Ich stelle das Glas neben das Hotel. (Akkusativ: WHERE IS IT GOING? → movement — unusual at A1 but shows the contrast)

After any dative preposition, the articles become:
- Masculine noun: dem (der → dem)
- Feminine noun: der (die → der)
- Neuter noun: dem (das → dem)
- Plural noun: den (die → den)

Common contractions you will see constantly:
- in + dem = im   → Ich bin im Hotel.
- an + dem = am   → Er steht am Bahnhof.
- zu + dem = zum  → Ich gehe zum Restaurant.
- zu + der = zur  → Ich gehe zur Apotheke.

sections.title: Common Exceptions
sections.content:
1. Plural nouns in Dativ add -n to the noun itself (if the plural form doesn't already end in -n or -s):
   - die Männer → den Männern
   - die Kinder → den Kindern
   - die Hotels → den Hotels (no extra -n, already ends in -s)

2. N-nouns (certain masculine nouns add -en in all cases except Nominativ):
   - der Herr → den Herrn (Akk) → dem Herrn (Dat)
   These are not very common at A1 level but good to recognize.

transfer_notes: Spanish has gender (el/la) but only 2 genders. German has 3 (m/f/n), and the article changes with case — don't try to map Spanish el/la directly to German der/die/das.
Spanish also uses indirect objects (le doy el libro a él), which closely parallels Dativ. The concept is the same; the mechanism differs (Spanish uses clitic pronouns, German uses article changes).
English has no article changes for case at all — 'the' and 'a' never change form. This is why case feels invisible to English speakers: you must mentally track each noun's grammatical role.
The Spanish 'personal a' (marking a direct-object person: 'veo a Juan') has no equivalent in German — German uses Akkusativ article forms (den/einen/keinen) to signal the direct object.
explanation.key_takeaway: In Akkusativ only the masculine article changes (der → den, ein → einen, kein → keinen); in Dativ ALL genders change, and the same changes apply whether the dative comes from an indirect object or from a dative preposition like vor, neben, or an.

examples.grammar_note: Nominativ masculine: 'der' — Zug is the subject performing no action but being described.
Akkusativ masculine indefinite: ein → einen — Kaffee is the direct object of kaufen.
Nominativ neuter: 'kein' — Hotel is neuter (das Hotel); kein does not change in Nominativ neuter.

examples.english: I don't see the train station.
examples.grammar_note: Akkusativ masculine definite: der → den — Bahnhof is the direct object of sehen.

examples.english: She is not hungry. (lit. She has no hunger.)
examples.grammar_note: Akkusativ masculine: kein → keinen — Hunger is masculine (der Hunger) and the direct object of haben.

examples.german: Das Café ist neben dem Hotel.
examples.english: The café is next to the hotel.
examples.grammar_note: Dativ neuter after 'neben' (location): das → dem — Hotel is neuter and the location is fixed (not a direction).

examples.german: Ich gebe dem Kellner ein Trinkgeld.
examples.english: I give the waiter a tip.
examples.grammar_note: Double object: 'dem Kellner' = Dativ masculine (indirect object, der → dem); 'ein Trinkgeld' = Akkusativ neuter indefinite (no change for neuter).

examples.german: Das Restaurant ist neben der Apotheke und vor dem Bahnhof.
examples.english: The restaurant is next to the pharmacy and in front of the train station.
examples.grammar_note: Two location prepositions, both forcing Dativ: 'neben der Apotheke' (feminine die → der) and 'vor dem Bahnhof' (masculine der → dem).

examples.german: Er hat kein Ticket gekauft, weil er kein Geld hat.
examples.english: He didn't buy a ticket because he has no money.
examples.grammar_note: Akkusativ neuter: 'kein Ticket' and 'kein Geld' — both neuter nouns, Akkusativ of kein = kein (no change for neuter).

examples.german: Ich wohne in einem Hotel in der Stadt.
examples.english: I live in a hotel in the city.
examples.grammar_note: Dativ after 'in' (location): 'einem Hotel' (neuter indefinite: ein → einem) and 'der Stadt' (feminine definite: die → der).

examples.german: Sie sucht eine Apotheke, aber sie findet keine.
examples.english: She is looking for a pharmacy, but she doesn't find one.
examples.grammar_note: Akkusativ feminine: 'eine Apotheke' and 'keine' — feminine indefinite Akkusativ stays eine/keine (no change for feminine).

examples.german: Er hat dem Taxifahrer den Bahnhof gezeigt.
examples.english: He showed the taxi driver the train station.
examples.grammar_note: Perfekt with double object: 'dem Taxifahrer' (Dativ masculine indirect object) + 'den Bahnhof' (Akkusativ masculine direct object).

exercises.prompt: ___ Zug ist schnell. (The train — Nominativ, masculine)
exercises.explanation: Nominativ masculine definite article = 'der'. Zug (train) is the subject of the sentence.

exercises.prompt: Ich kaufe ___ Brot. (a bread — Akkusativ, neuter)
exercises.explanation: Akkusativ neuter indefinite = 'ein'. Neuter nouns do not change between Nominativ and Akkusativ.

exercises.prompt: Das Café ist neben ___ Bahnhof. (the train station — Dativ, masculine)
exercises.explanation: 'Neben' for location forces Dativ. Masculine definite article in Dativ = 'dem'.

exercises.prompt: Er hat ___ Hunger. (no hunger — Akkusativ, masculine)
exercises.explanation: Hunger is masculine (der Hunger) and the direct object of haben (Akkusativ). Kein → keinen in Akkusativ masculine.

exercises.prompt: Ich gebe ___ Kellner ___ Trinkgeld. (the waiter — Dativ masc. / a tip — Akkusativ neuter)
exercises.explanation: 'Dem Kellner' = Dativ masculine definite (indirect object). 'Ein Trinkgeld' = Akkusativ neuter indefinite (neuter has no change in Akkusativ).

exercises.prompt:
Das ist ___ Hotel.
a) ein
b) einen
c) einem
exercises.explanation: Hotel is neuter (das Hotel). Nominativ neuter indefinite = 'ein'. 'Einen' is masculine Akkusativ; 'einem' is Dativ.

exercises.prompt:
Das ist ___ Apotheke.
a) ein
b) einen
c) eine
exercises.explanation: Apotheke is feminine (die Apotheke). Nominativ feminine indefinite = 'eine'. 'Ein' is masculine/neuter; 'einen' is masculine Akkusativ.

exercises.prompt:
Ich sehe ___ Mann nicht.
a) der
b) den
c) dem
exercises.explanation: Mann is the direct object (Akkusativ), masculine. Masculine definite Akkusativ = 'den'. 'Der' is Nominativ; 'dem' is Dativ.

exercises.prompt:
Das Buch liegt auf ___ Tisch. (location — where is the book?)
a) der
b) den
c) dem
exercises.explanation: 'Auf' for location forces Dativ. Tisch is masculine (der Tisch), so Dativ masculine definite = 'dem'. If it expressed direction (putting the book onto the table), it would be Akkusativ: 'den Tisch'.
'Frau' is Nominativ subject (die Frau). 'Auto' is neuter (das Auto), Akkusativ direct object of haben. Neuter Akkusativ of kein = 'kein' (no change).

exercises.prompt: I am looking for the train station.
exercises.explanation: 'Bahnhof' is masculine (der Bahnhof), Akkusativ direct object of suchen. Masculine definite Akkusativ = 'den'.

exercises.prompt: The restaurant is next to the pharmacy.
exercises.solution: Das Restaurant ist neben der Apotheke.
exercises.explanation: 'Neben' for location forces Dativ. Apotheke is feminine (die Apotheke). Feminine definite Dativ = 'der'.

exercises.prompt: He has bought no ticket. (Use Perfekt: kaufen → hat gekauft)
exercises.explanation: 'Ticket' is neuter (das Ticket), Akkusativ direct object. Neuter Akkusativ of kein = 'kein' (no change). Perfekt: hat + past participle at the end.
'Kaffee' is the direct object (Akkusativ) and masculine. Masculine definite Akkusativ = 'den', not 'der' (which is Nominativ masculine).

exercises.prompt: Das Hotel ist neben die Apotheke.
exercises.solution: Das Hotel ist neben der Apotheke.
exercises.explanation: 'Neben' for location forces Dativ. Apotheke is feminine. Feminine definite Dativ = 'der', not 'die' (which is Nominativ/Akkusativ feminine).

exercises.prompt: Ich habe kein Hunger, aber ich esse ein Sandwich.
exercises.solution: Ich habe keinen Hunger, aber ich esse ein Sandwich.
exercises.explanation: 'Hunger' is masculine (der Hunger) and the direct object of haben (Akkusativ). Kein → keinen in Akkusativ masculine. The second clause is correct: Sandwich is neuter, and neuter Akkusativ of ein = 'ein' (no change).
